name: dkist-processing-math

metadata.name: dkist-processing-math
metadata.summary: Common math functionality used in the DKIST calibrations suite

documentation:
# dkist-processing-math

dkist-processing-math contains the math functions used throughout the DKIST data processing software stack.

## Development

```bash
git clone git@bitbucket.org:dkistdc/dkist-processing-visp.git
cd dkist-processing-visp
pre-commit install
pip install -e .[test]
pytest -v --cov dkist_processing_visp
```

### Changelog

When you make **any** change to this repository it **MUST** be accompanied by a changelog file.
The changelog for this repository uses the [towncrier](https://github.com/twisted/towncrier) package.
Entries in the changelog for the next release are added as individual files (one per change) to the `changelog/` directory.

#### Writing a Changelog Entry

A changelog entry accompanying a change should be added to the `changelog/` directory.
The name of a file in this directory follows a specific template:

```
<PULL REQUEST NUMBER>.<TYPE>[.<COUNTER>].rst
```

The fields have the following meanings:

- `<PULL REQUEST NUMBER>`: This is the number of the pull request, so people can jump from the changelog entry to the diff on BitBucket.
- `<TYPE>`: This is the type of the change and must be one of the values described below.
- `<COUNTER>`: This is an optional field, if you make more than one change of the same type you can append a counter to the subsequent changes, i.e. `100.bugfix.rst` and `100.bugfix.1.rst` for two bugfix changes in the same PR.

The list of possible types is defined the the towncrier section of `pyproject.toml`, the types are:

- `feature`: This change is a new code feature.
- `bugfix`: This is a change which fixes a bug.
- `doc`: A documentation change.
- `removal`: A deprecation or removal of public API.
- `misc`: Any small change which doesn't fit anywhere else, such as a change to the package infrastructure.

#### Rendering the Changelog at Release Time

When you are about to tag a release first you must run `towncrier` to render the changelog.
The steps for this are as follows:

- Run `towncrier build --version vx.y.z` using the version number you want to tag.
- Agree to have towncrier remove the fragments.
- Add and commit your changes.
- Tag the release.

**NOTE:** If you forget to add a Changelog entry to a tagged release (either manually or automatically with `towncrier`)
then the Bitbucket pipeline will fail. To be able to use the same tag you must delete it locally and on the remote branch:

```bash
# First, actually update the CHANGELOG and commit the update
git commit

# Delete tags
git tag -d vWHATEVER.THE.VERSION
git push --delete origin vWHATEVER.THE.VERSION

# Re-tag with the same version
git tag vWHATEVER.THE.VERSION
git push --tags origin main
```

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.feature
documentation: Feature finding.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.feature.find_px_angles
documentation:
Find the most significant angles in a Hough transform.

Peaks in the Hough transform are first found with a simple max filter and then refined via interpolation of the
surrounding peak.

Parameters
----------
hough_accumulator
    A 2D array representing a Hough accumulator

theta
    A 1D array of the angle values corresponding to hough_accumulator

peak_func
    Function that will be called to detect a unique peak in the x,y data. Must have args (x, y) and return x peak value.

px_width
    The width, in pixels, around (+/-) the initial peak to apply peak_func for peak refinement

raise_error_on_failed_interpolation
    If True, raise an error if the peak refinement minimization fails. Otherwise return the non-refined peak in this case.

Returns
-------
peak_theta
    The most significant angles found

modules.name: docs.conf
documentation: Configuration file for the Sphinx documentation builder.

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binary
documentation: Functionality for binary images.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binary.make_binary
documentation:
Generate a binary image from an input float or int array.

A threshold value is chosen via Otsu's method and all values below the threshold are set to 1 while everything
else is set to 0. Thus the result is technically an _inverse_ binary image, which is useful for isolating the
target grid as a feature of interest.

Parameters
----------
data
    Data to convert to binary

numotsu
    The number of times to perform thresholding using Otsu's method. Numbers larger than 1 are useful if the
    pixel distribution of the data has > 2 modes.

Returns
-------
An array of 1's and 0's corresponding to the thresholded input data.

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.transform
documentation: init.

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine
documentation: Affine transforms.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine.affine_transform_arrays
documentation:
Transform an iterable of input arrays using a generalized affine transform operator.

A transform matrix may be specified or separate parameters for scale, translation,
rotation and shear may be specified instead. This method abstracts the use of the
scikit-image affine transform, which may easily be replaced with one from scipy or
another origin. If transform parameters are specified, they are applied in this order:
shear, scale, rotation, translation

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The array(s) to be transformed
matrix
    The transformation matrix to be used for the transform. If specified, none of
    [translation, rotation, or shear] may be used. Optional. If matrix is not specified,
    at least one of the following must be used: scale, translation, rotation, shear
scale
    The scale factor to be applied in the transform: (Sx, Sy). If a scalar is used, the same
    value is applied to each axis.
translation
    The translation to be applied in the transform: (Tx, Ty) in pixel units.
rotation
    The rotation angle, in radians, to be applied to the transformation.
    A positive angle is counter-clockwise in a right handed coordinate system
shear
    The shear factor to be applied in the transform: (Shx, Shy). If a scalar is used, the same
    value is applied to each axis.
**kwargs
    Optional arguments to be passed on to skimage.transform.warp() if desired. See
    https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.transform.html#skimage.transform.warp
    for more details.

Returns
-------
The transformed array(s)

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine.rotate_arrays_about_point
documentation:
Rotate an iterable of arrays about a specified point.

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The array(s) to be rotated

angle
    The angle, in radians, for the rotation to be applied, optional. Default is zero
    A positive angle is counter-clockwise in a right handed coordinate system and
    clockwise in a left-handed coordinate system

point
    The point, in pixel coordinates (x, y) about which to rotate. Optional. Default is None,
    meaning the center of the array will be used. Note that the origin pixel is centered at
    (0, 0) and has extent: [-0.5: 0.5, -0.5: 0.5]

kwargs
    Any arguments to be passed to skimage.transform.rotate. See
    https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.transform.html#skimage.transform.rotate
    for more details.

Returns
-------
An Iterator yielding the rotated arrays.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine.translate_arrays
documentation:
Translate an iterable of arrays by a specified vector.

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The array(s) to be translated

translation
    The translation to be applied in the transform: (Tx, Ty) in pixel units. Optional.

**kwargs
    Optional arguments to be passed on to skimage.transform.warp() if desired. See
    https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.transform.html#skimage.transform.warp
    for more details.

Returns
-------
np.ndarray
    The translated array

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine.scale_matrix
documentation:
Create an Affine Transform matrix for a scale operation.

Parameters
----------
scale_factors
    The scale factors to use in scaling the array. If a scalar is passed, it
    is applied to both axes.

Returns
-------
A 3x3 homogeneous transform matrix implementing the scaling

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine.rotation_matrix
documentation:
Create an Affine Transform matrix for a rotation operation.

This assumes the rotation is about (0, 0) and it is up to the user
to calculate the appropriate translation offset to be applied in the
skimage.warp() method to achieve rotation about an arbitrary point

Parameters
----------
angle
    The angle of rotation in radians. This represents counter-clockwise rotation
    in a right-handed coordinate system and clockwise rotation in a left-handed
    system

Returns
-------
A 3x3 homogeneous transform matrix implementing the rotation

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine.shear_matrix
documentation:
Create an Affine Transform matrix for a shear operation.

Parameters
----------
shear_factors
    The shear scaling factors.

Returns
-------
A 3x3 homogeneous transform matrix implementing the shear

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.affine.translation_matrix
documentation:
Create an Affine Transform matrix for a translation operation.

Parameters
----------
offsets
    The translation ofsets.

Returns
-------
A 3x3 homogeneous transform matrix implementing the translation

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.hough
documentation: Hough transforms.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.hough.do_hough
documentation:
Perform a Hough line transform on input data.

Resolution in the angle dimension can be increased via numtheta.

Parameters
----------
binary
    An integer array of 1's and 0's

theta_min
    The minimum angle to use for the hough transform fitting

theta_max
    The maximum angle to use for the hough transform fitting

numtheta
    The number of samples in the theta dimension of the Hough transform.

Returns
-------
A tuple containing:
    - The 2D Hough space (i.e., accumulator) of the input image.
    - A 1D array of the angle values corresponding to the Hough accumulator. Angles will range from -pi/4 to 3/4 pi and the length will be equal to numtheta.
    - A 1D array of the distance values corresponding to the Hough accumulator. This array is entirely determined by skimage.transform.hough_line

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binning

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binning.bin_arrays
documentation:
Bin an iterable of arrays by summation over an integral number of pixels in each dimension.

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The arrays to be binned

bin_factors
    The bin factors for each axis, which must be integral divisors of each array dimension

Returns
-------
An Iterator yielding the binned arrays

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binning._bin_array
documentation:
Bin an array by summation over an integral number of pixels in each dimension.

Parameters
----------
array
    The array to be binned

bin_factors
    The bin factors for each axis, which must be integral divisors of each array dimension

Returns
-------
The binned array

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binning.resize_arrays
documentation:
Resize an iterable of arrays using specified interpolation order.

This function is more geared towards up-sampling arrays smoothly. For more accurate downsampling see
`resize_arrays_local_mean`

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The arrays to be binned

output_shape
    Shape to bin the input arrays to

order
    The order of interpolation:

        0: Nearest-neighbor
        1: Bi-linear (default)
        2: Bi-quadratic
        3: Bi-cubic
        4: Bi-quartic
        5: Bi-quintic

mode
    Points outside the boundaries of the input are filled according to the given mode. See `numpy.pad` for explanations.

Returns
-------
An Iterator yielding the binned arrays

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binning._resize_array
documentation:
Resize an array using the given interpolation order.

Parameters
----------
array
    The array to be binned

output_shape
    Shape to bin the input arrays to

order
    The order of interpolation:

        0: Nearest-neighbor
        1: Bi-linear (default)
        2: Bi-quadratic
        3: Bi-cubic
        4: Bi-quartic
        5: Bi-quintic

mode
    Points outside the boundaries of the input are filled according to the given mode. See `numpy.pad` for explanations.

Returns
-------
The binned array

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binning.resize_arrays_local_mean
documentation:
Bin an iterable of arrays by summation over an integral number of pixels in each dimension.

This function is good for downsampling arrays, but acts like nearest-neighbor when upsampling.
See `resize` for more flexible upsampling options.

Note that when downsampling this method is sensitive to extreme outliers.

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The arrays to be binned

output_shape
    Shape to bin the input arrays to

Returns
-------
An Iterator yielding the binned arrays

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.transform.binning._resize_array_local_mean
documentation:
Bin an array by summation over an integral number of pixels in each dimension.

Parameters
----------
array
    The array to be binned

output_shape
    Shape to bin the input arrays to

Returns
-------
The binned array

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.linear_algebra
documentation: Linear algebra functions.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.linear_algebra.nd_left_matrix_multiply
documentation:
Left-multiply an arbitrarily dimensioned stack of vectors by a similarly dimensioned stack of matrices.

In math:

    result = M @ v

where M is a matrix with dimensions ([n1, ...nn], D1, D2)
and v is a vector with dimensions ([n1, ...nn], D2).

The higher-order dimensions (n1, ...nn) can be anything (or nothing), but they must be the same for M and v.
A special case where either the matrix or vector has NO higher-order dimensions is also allowed; the result will be
the single matrix/vector broadcast over the higher-order dimensionality of the other element.

Processing inputs with different numbers of higher-order dummy dimensions (i.e., dimension of length 1), while
technically possible, is not supported because it would be unclear which input should set the output shape.

Parameters
----------
vector_stack : np.ndarray
    ([n1, ...nn], D2) ND stack of vectors with length D2

matrix_stack : np.ndarray
    ([n1, ...nn], D1, D2) ND stack of matrices with shape (D1, D2)


Returns
-------
np.ndarray
    ([n1, ...nn], D1) ND stack of vectors with length D1

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.linear_algebra.nd_right_matrix_multiply
documentation:
Right-multiply an arbitrarily dimensioned stack of vectors by a similarly dimensioned stack of matrices.

In math:

    result = v @ M

where M is a matrix with dimensions ([n1, ...nn], D1, D2)
and v is a vector with dimensions ([n1, ...nn], D1).

The higher-order dimensions (n1, ...nn) can be anything (or nothing), but they must be the same for M and v.
A special case where either the matrix or vector has NO higher-order dimensions is also allowed; the result will be
the single matrix/vector broadcast over the higher-order dimensionality of the other element.

Processing inputs with different numbers of higher-order dummy dimensions (i.e., dimension of length 1), while
technically possible, is not supported because it would be unclear which input should set the output shape.

Parameters
----------
vector_stack : np.ndarray
    ([n1, ...nn], D2) ND stack of vectors with length D1

matrix_stack : np.ndarray
    ([n1, ...nn], D1, D2) ND stack of matrices with shape (D1, D2)


Returns
-------
np.ndarray
    ([n1, ...nn], D1) ND stack of vectors with length D2

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.linear_algebra._check_nd_dimensionality
documentation:
Check that the matrix and vector stacks have higher-order dimensions that can be broadcast together.

Enforces matrix stack shape[:-2] equals vector stack shape[:-1]. Raises a ValueError if the condition is not met.

A special case of a matrix or vector with no higher-order dimensions is allowed.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.tests.test_linear_algebra.test_nd_left_matrix_multiply
documentation:
Given: Matrix and vector stacks with arbitrary higher dimensions
When: Left matrix multiplying them
Then: The matrix multiplication is correctly broadcast across the higher dimensions
Stopping at 7 for no real reason. We need 0 and 2 for sure and then others to confirm generality.
single_matrix = True tests the case where the matrix has no higher dimensions. Probably only need to test it once
but we do it twice just because.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.tests.test_linear_algebra.test_nd_left_matrix_multiply_errors
documentation:
Given: Matrix and vector stacks with mismatched shapes
When: Trying to matrix multiply them
Then: The correct error is raised

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.tests.test_linear_algebra.test_nd_right_matrix_multiply
documentation:
Given: Matrix and vector stacks with arbitrary higher dimensions
When: Right matrix multiplying them
Then: The matrix multiplication is correctly broadcast across the higher dimensions
Stopping at 7 for no real reason. We need 0 and 2 for sure and then others to confirm generality.
single_matrix = True tests the case where the matrix has no higher dimensions. Probably only need to test it once
but we do it twice just because.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.tests.test_linear_algebra.test_nd_right_matrix_multiply_errors
documentation:
Given: Matrix and vector stacks with mismatched shapes
When: Trying to matrix multiply them
Then: The correct error is raised

modules.name: dkist_processing_math.arithmetic
documentation: Arithmetic.

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.arithmetic.subtract_array_from_arrays
documentation:
Subtract a single array from an iterable of arrays.

This will work if a single array is used in lieu of an iterable as well.

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The arrays from which to subtract
array_to_subtract
    The array to be subtracted

Returns
-------
An generator of modified arrays

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.arithmetic.subtract_array_from_fits_access
documentation:
Subtract a single array from an iterable of FitsAccess objects.

This will work if a single object is used in lieu of an iterable as well. The objects themselves are modified in place.

Parameters
----------
access_objs
    The objects from which to subtract

array_to_subtract
    The array to be subtracted

Returns
-------
An generator of modified FitsAccess objects

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.arithmetic.divide_arrays_by_array
documentation:
Divide an iterable of arrays by a single array.

This will work if a single array is used in lieu of an iterable as well.

Parameters
----------
arrays
    The arrays to be divided
array_to_divide_by
    The array by which to divide

Returns
-------
A generator of modified arrays

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.arithmetic.divide_fits_access_by_array
documentation:
Divide an iterable of FitsAccess objects by a single array.

This will work if a single object is used in lieu of an iterable as well. The objects themselves are modified in place.

Parameters
----------
access_objs
    The FitsAccess objects to be divided

array_to_divide_by
    The array by which to divide

Returns
-------
A generator of modified FitsAccess objects

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.tests.test_transform.test_affine_transform_specify_shear
documentation:
Given: a numpy_array
When: applying the affine transform and specifying a shear angle
Then: an Iterator containing the sheared array is returned

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.tests.test_transform.test_make_binary_trigger_cutoff

Then: make_binary returns before a single value array is created

functions.name: dkist_processing_math.tests.test_transform.test_do_hough
documentation:
